theptwelve
5
 
●
 
INTRODUCTION
 
 
“Visayan”,
 
“Bisaya”,
 
“Binisaya”,
 
“Sinugboanon”,
 
“Cebuano”,
 
“Sinibwano”
 
All
 
names
 
for
 
the
 
same
 
language!
 
 
 
Cebuano
 
is
 
a
 
Visayan
 
language
 
spoken
 
by
 
more
 
than
 
20
 
million
 
people
 
or
 
approximately
 
22%
 
of
 
the
 
Philippine
 
population.
 
Comparatively,
 
Tagalog
 
is
 
spoken
 
by
 
around
 
25
 
million
 
first-language
 
speakers.
 
Cebuano
 
is
 
the
 
second
 
most
 
spoken
 
language
 
in
 
the
 
Philippines
 
and
 
is
 
used
 
as
 
a
 
lingua
 
franca
 
in
 
many
 
parts
 
of
 
the
 
southern
 
Philippines.
 
It
 
has
 
slightly
 
more
 
speakers
 
than
 
Norwegian,
 
Swedish,
 
and
 
Danish
 
combined.
 
 
Cebuano
 
is
 
part
 
of
 
the
 
extensive
 
Austronesian
 
language
 
family,
 
which
 
shares
 
common
 
ancestry
 
with
 
languages
 
such
 
as
 
Tagalog,
 
Malaysian,
 
Indonesian,
 
Maori,
 
Hawaiian,
 
and
 
Malagasy.
 
It
 
is
 
very
 
similar
 
to
 
other
 
Visayan
 
languages
 
such
 
as
 
Waray-waray
 
(Samarnon/Samareño)
 
and
 
Ilonggo
 
(Hiligaynon).
 
Due
 
to
 
Spain’s
 
colonization
 
of
 
the
 
Philippines
 
which
 
spanned
 
333
 
years,
 
there
 
are
 
many
 
Spanish
 
loan
 
words.
 
The
 
pronunciation
 
of
 
these
 
loan
 
words
 
tend
 
to
 
be
 
slightly
 
different
 
from
 
the
 
Spanish
 
and
 
they
 
may
 
have
 
experienced
 
meaning
 
change
 
over
 
the
 
centuries.
 
 
Different
 
areas
 
of
 
the
 
Cebuano
 
speaking
 
world
 
have
 
their
 
own
 
unique
 
accents
 
and
 
innovations
 
which
 
can
 
be
 
recognizable
 
to
 
speakers
 
from
 
other
 
regions.
 
It
 
is
 
similar
 
to
 
the
 
difference
 
in
 
accents
 
from
 
the
 
different
 
regions
 
of
 
the
 
USA
 
or
 
the
 
UK.
 
For
 
this
 
class,
 
we
 
will
 
primarily
 
use
 
the
 
South
 
Cebu
 
dialect
 
for
 
spelling
 
which
 
is
 
hailed
 
as
 
the
 
standard
 
by
 
many
 
language
 
organizations
 
including
 
The
 
Philippine
 
Bibles
 
Society
 
which
 
distributes
 
copies
 
of
 
the
 
Cebuano
 
Bible
 
in
 
the
 
region.
 
This
 
dialect
 
is
 
considered
 
to
 
be
 
more
 
conservative
 
and
 
lacks
 
the
 
notorious
 
“shortcuts”
 
of
 
the
 
Cebu
 
City
 
(North
 
Cebu)
 
dialect
 
and
 
is
 
used
 
widely
 
throughout
 
the
 
southern
 
Philippines.
 
It
 
is
 
also
 
the
 
preferred
 
dialect
 
in
 
generally
 
formal,
 
religious
 
and
 
political
 
settings.
 
 
There
 
can
 
be
 
marked
 
differences
 
in
 
pronunciation
 
among
 
the
 
different
 
social
 
classes.
 
Those
 
with
 
a
 
high
 
level
 
of
 
education
 
and/or
 
social
 
class
 
may
 
switch
 
in
 
and
 
out
 
of
 
English
 
frequently,
 
even
 
in
 
the
 
same
 
sentence.
 
“I
 
don’t
 
know
 
kung
 
nganong
 
ingon
 
ana
 
na
 
siyá.”
 
(I
 
don’t
 
know
 
why
 
he’s
 
like
 
that.)
 
Cebuano
 
as
 
spoken
 
in
 
the
 
countryside
 
(in
 
“the
 
province”
 
as
 
it
 
is
 
here
 
called)
 
and
 
among
 
the
 
poorer
 
classes
 
tends
 
to
 
be
 
“rougher”
 
sounding
 
and
 
there
 
is
 
much
 
less
 
English
 
mixed
 
into
 
the
 
language.
 
They
 
also
 
may
 
use
 
words
 
considered
 
“deep”
 
by
 
the
 
social
 
elites
 
of
 
the
 
Visayan
 
world.
 
 
The
 
language’s
 
variety
 
in
 
names
 
is
 
mainly
 
because
 
of
 
technically
 
overlapping
 
definitions
 
that
 
lead
 
to
 
ambiguity.
 
Many
 
have
 
been
 
proposing
 
names
 
but
 
most
 
are
 
not
 
truly
 
widely
 
accepted
 
considering
 
the
 
resistance
 
of
 
the
 
other
 
speakers
 
outside
 
of
 
Cebu.
 
Native
 
speakers
 
have
 
been
 
normally
 
calling
 
the
 
language
 
“Bisaya”
 
or
 
“Binisaya”
 
(literally
 
“the
 
Visayan
 
manner”)
 
in
 
the
 
native
 
tongue
 
which
 
translates
 
to
 
English
 
as
 
“Bisayan”
 
or
 
“Visayan”.
 
The
 
terms
 
“Cebuano”,
 
“Sinibwano”,
 
“Sinugboanon”
 
(literally
 
“of
 
the
 
Cebuano
 
manner”)
 
are
 
formally
 
used
 
to
 
distinguish
 
it
 
from
 
the
 
other
 
Visayan
 
languages
 
in
 
the
 
Visayas
 
group
 
of
 
islands
 
which
 
also
 
had
 
a
 
history
 
of
 
calling
 
themselves
 
and
 
their
 
native
 
tongues
 
“Bisaya”
 
or
 
“Binisaya”.
 
The
 
Cebuan
 
group
 
has
 
been
 
historically
 
the
 
most
 
numerous
 
among
 
the
 
Visayans
 
and
 
when
 
the
 
use
 
of
 
these
 
terms
 
died
 
down
 
in
 
the
 
others,
 
they
 
became
 
associated
 
with
 
the
 
Cebuan
 
group
 
especially
 
in
 
mainstream
 
and
 
social
 
media
 
and
 
in
 
the
 
younger
 
generations
 
who
 
were
 
not
 
able
 
to
 
witness
 
a
 
time
 
when
 
all
 
of
 
the
 
Visayans
 
identified
 
as
 
Visayan.
 
 

theptwelve
6
 
●
 
PRONUNCIATION
 
GUIDE
 
 
VOWELS
 
 
 
Cebuano
 
has
 
only
 
3
 
vowels
 
/a/,
 
/i/,
 
and
 
/u/.
 
The
 
additional
 
two
 
/e/
 
and
 
/o/
 
are
 
found
 
in
 
loanwords
 
and
 
are
 
allophones
 
of
 
/i/
 
and
 
/u/.
 
In
 
spelling,
 
/e/
 
and
 
/o/
 
are
 
typed
 
out
 
but
 
are
 
pronounced
 
more
 
“hardly”
 
than
 
its
 
original
 
Spanish
 
&
 
English
 
pronunciations.
 
“Low”,
 
“mid”
 
and
 
“high”
 
indicate
 
tongue
 
height
 
whereas
 
“front”,
 
“central”,
 
“back”
 
indicate
 
the
 
portion
 
of
 
the
 
tongue
 
that
 
is
 
raised.

CONSONANTS
 
 
 
Cebuano
 
has
 
16
 
consonants
 
including
 
2
 
semivowels.
 
They
 
are
 
further
 
classified
 
with
 
articulatory
 
and
 
acoustic
 
descriptions.
 
Articulatory
 
description
 
is
 
all
 
about
 
describing
 
the
 
place
 
of
 
articulation
 
in
 
the
 
mouth.
 
Acoustic
 
description
 
describes
 
how
 
the
 
sound
 
is
 
produced
 
in
 
the
 
mouth.

Labial
 
(lips)

b,
 
m

(alveolar

●
 
ORTHOGRAPHY
 
&
 
SPELLING
 
 
The
 
Spanish
 
and
 
English
 
and
 
letters
 
C,
 
F,
 
LL,
 
Ñ,
 
PH,
 
Q,
 
V,
 
X,
 
Z
 
are
 
not
 
found
 
in
 
the
 
Cebuano-Visayan
 
alphabet
 
except
 
in
 
proper
 
nouns
 
especially
 
in
 
Filipino
 
names.
 
The
 
first
 
names
 
are
 
usually
 
in
 
English
 
while
 
the
 
surnames
 
are
 
mostly
 
of
 
Spanish
 
origin.
 
J
 
is
 
used
 
only
 
in
 
the
 
Bol-anon
 
variety
 
of
 
Cebuano.
 
Otherwise,
 
the
 
/dʒ/
 
sound
 
in
 
Cebuano
 
is
 
represented
 
by
 
the
 
letters
 
DY.
 
On
 
the
 
same
 
note,
 
the
 
English
 
/tʃ/
 
sound
 
in
 
“chair”
 
is
 
expressed
 
as
 
TS
 
in
 
Cebuano
 
while
 
the
 
/ʃ/
 
sound
 
in
 
“ship”
 
is
 
expressed
 
as
 
SY.

SPANISH
 
&
 
CEBUANO
 
ORTHOGRAPHY
 
 
 
The
 
Spanish
 
left
 
a
 
lot
 
of
 
loanwords
 
in
 
Cebuano.
 
Aside
 
from
 
this,
 
traces
 
of
 
their
 
orthography
 
are
 
still
 
evident
 
today.
 
It
 
is
 
handy
 
to
 
be
 
acquainted
 
with
 
the
 
way
 
sounds
 
are
 
symbolized
 
in
 
the
 
Spanish
 
orthography
 
for
 
you
 
to
 
be
 
able
 
to
 
read
 
someone’s
 
surname,
 
the
 
name
 
of
 
a
 
town,
 
or
 
a
 
dish.
 
 
Each
 
Spanish
 
spelling
 
is
 
demonstrated
 
below
 
and
 
their
 
modern
 
Cebuano
 
equivalents
 
are
 
given
 
after.
 
There
 
hasn’t
 
been
 
a
 
universally
 
accepted
 
standard
 
orthography
 
in
 
Cebuano
 
despite
 
many
 
attempts.
 
An
 
“official”
 
one
 
assisted
 
by
 
the
 
government
 
is
 
used
 
in
 
MTB-MLE
 
(Mother
 
Tongue
 
Based
 
-
 
Multilingual
 
Education),
 
a
 
subject
 
taught
 
in
 
elementary
 
schools
 
from
 
the
 
1st
 
to
 
the
 
3rd
 
grade.
 
Choices
 
and
 
preferences
 
of
 
the
 
said
 
orthography
 
as
 
well
 
as
 
the
 
ones
 
used
 
in
 
this
 
book
 
are
 
discussed
 
below.
 
 
1.
 
I
 
and
 
Y
 
(front
 
 
 
The
 
/i/
 
sound
 
at
 
the
 
beginning
 
of
 
a
 
word
 
or
 
after
 
a
 
glottal
 
stop
 
was
 
written
 
as
 
I
 
or
 
Y
 
interchangeably.
 
In
 
modern
 
Cebuano,
 
this
 
is
 
consistently
 
spelled
 
as
 
I
 
except
 
when
 
it
 
is
 
after
 
the
 
semivowel
 
Y.

-
 
ingón
 
(say/tell)

isdà
 
(fish)

-
 
ítlog
 
(egg)
 
 
 
ybog

(attract,

(a

marker)

to

from

a

that
 
means
 
“and”.
 
 
In
 
earlier
 
attempts
 
for
 
a
 
standard
 
orthography,
 
the
 
E
 
and
 
O

in

in

An

NUMBERS
 
 
 
The
 
native
 
numbers
 
are
 
used
 
from
 
1-10.
 
The
 
rest
 
are
 
in
 
Spanish
 
except
 
for
 
multiples
 
of
 
100
 
and
 
1000.
 
This
 
is
 
the
 
usual
 
way
 
of
 
doing
 
it

the
 
native
 
and
 
Spanish
 
numbers

also
 
used
 
in
 
the
 
other
 
cases
 
but
 
only
 
in
 
rare
 
occasions.
 
The
 
English
 
numbers
 
have
 
become
 
increasingly
 
preferred
 
by
 
the
 
younger
 
generations.
 
It’s
 
been
 
joked
 
a

that

not
 
required.

AUSTRONESIAN
 
ALIGNMENT
 
 
 
Cebuano
 
is
 
a
 
Philippine-type
 
Austronesian
 
language
 
which
 
means
 
that
 
it
 
retained
 
the
 
four-voiced
 
alignment
 
unlike
 
other
 
Austronesian
 
languages
 
such
 
as
 
Malay,
 
Maori
 
or
 
Hawaiian.
 
Austronesian
 
alignment
 
differs
 
from
 
the
 
morphosyntactic
 
alignment
 
in
 
other
 
languages
 
such
 
as
 
English.
 
English
 
follows
 
a
 
nominative-accusative
 
alignment
 
where
 
the
 
subject
 
of
 
an
 
intransitive
 
verb
 
is
 
marked
 
similarly
 
to
 
the
 
agent
 
of
 
a
 
transitive
 
verb.
 
In
 
an
 
ergative-accusative
 
alignment
 
the
 
subject
 
of
 
the
 
intransitive
 
verb
 
and
 
the
 
object
 
of
 
a
 
transitive
 
verb
 
are
 
both
 
identified
 
by
 
the
 
same
 
marker.
 
 
 
 
 
 
Nominative-Accusative
 
 
Ergative-Absolutive
 
 
Inransitive:
 
 
She
 
saw.
 
 
 
 
She
 
saw.
 
 
 
Transitive:
 
 
She
 
saw
 
him
.
 
 
 
 
Her
 
saw
 
he
.
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
SUB-INT
 
=
 
SUB-TRA

=
 
OBJ=TRA
 
 
 
 
The
 
Austronesian
 
alignment
 
has
 
a
 
mix
 
of
 
both
 
and
 
more.
 
It
 
has
 
1
 
active
 
(agent)
 
and
 
3
 
passives
 
(patient,
 
referent
 
and
 
assistent).
 
These
 
four
 
are
 
usually
 
called
 
either
 
“focus”
 
or
 
“voice”.
 
The
 
verb
 
is
 
conjugated
 
with
 
an
 
affix
 
that
 
tells
 
the
 
role
 
of
 
the
 
focused
 
noun
 
in
 
a
 
particular
 
voice.
 
And
 
each
 
voice
 
has
 
its
 
own
 
sets
 
of
 
affixes
 
depending
 
on
 
the
 
aspect
 
and
 
mood.

Transitivity
 
&
 
Verbal
 
Substitution
 
 
TRANSITIVITY
 
 
Transitivity
 
is
 
a
 
grammatical
 
property
 
of
 
verbs
 
where
 
a
 
verb
 
needs
 
a
 
direct
 
object
 
in
 
transition
.
 
Therefore,
 
a
 
transitive
 
verb
 
has
 
an
 
agent
 
and
 
a
 
patient
 
(or
 
non-agent)
 
involved.
 
An
 
intransitive
 
verb
 
only
 
needs
 
one
 
role
 
-
 
the
 
agent.
 
It
 
does
 
not
 
need
 
a
 
patient
 
for
 
the
 
action
 
to
 
be
 
actualized.
 
This
 
is
 
called
 
“unergative”,
 
an
 
intransitive
 
verb
 
that
 
lacks
 
an
 
object
 
or
 
patient.
 
However,
 
there
 
are
 
also
 
intransitive
 
verbs
 
that
 
lack
 
a
 
subject
 
or
 
agent
 
patient.
 
These
 
are
 
called
 
“unaccusative”
 
or
 
also
 
known
 
as
 
“change-of-state”
 
verbs.
 
Some
 
verbs
 
are
 
inherently
 
intransitive
 
or
 
transitive,
 
some
 
can
 
be
 
both.
 
Another
 
thing
 
to
 
take
 
note
 
of
 
is
 
that
 
Cebuano
 
is
 
mainly
 
a
 
VSO
 
(Verb
 
Subject
 
Object)
 
language
 
compared
 
to
 
English
 
being
 
mainly
 
an
 
SVO
 
(Subject
 
Verb
 
Object)
 
language.

5
 
●
 
INTRODUCTION
 
 
“Visayan”,
 
“Bisaya”,
 
“Binisaya”,
 
“Sinugboanon”,
 
“Cebuano”,
 
“Sinibwano”
 
All
 
names
 
for
 
the
 
same
 
language!
 
 
 
Cebuano
 
is
 
a
 
Visayan
 
language
 
spoken
 
by
 
more
 
than
 
20
 
million
 
people
 
or
 
approximately
 
22%
 
of
 
the
 
Philippine
 
population.
 
Comparatively,
 
Tagalog
 
is
 
spoken
 
by
 
around
 
25
 
million
 
first-language
 
speakers.
 
Cebuano
 
is
 
the
 
second
 
most
 
spoken
 
language
 
in
 
the
 
Philippines
 
and
 
is
 
used
 
as
 
a
 
lingua
 
franca
 
in
 
many
 
parts
 
of
 
the
 
southern
 
Philippines.
 
It
 
has
 
slightly
 
more
 
speakers
 
than
 
Norwegian,
 
Swedish,
 
and
 
Danish
 
combined.
 
 
Cebuano
 
is
 
part
 
of
 
the
 
extensive
 
Austronesian
 
language
 
family,
 
which
 
shares
 
common
 
ancestry
 
with
 
languages
 
such
 
as
 
Tagalog,
 
Malaysian,
 
Indonesian,
 
Maori,
 
Hawaiian,
 
and
 
Malagasy.
 
It
 
is
 
very
 
similar
 
to
 
other
 
Visayan
 
languages
 
such
 
as
 
Waray-waray
 
(Samarnon/Samareño)
 
and
 
Ilonggo
 
(Hiligaynon).
 
Due
 
to
 
Spain’s
 
colonization
 
of
 
the
 
Philippines
 
which
 
spanned
 
333
 
years,
 
there
 
are
 
many
 
Spanish
 
loan
 
words.
 
The
 
pronunciation
 
of
 
these
 
loan
 
words
 
tend
 
to
 
be
 
slightly
 
different
 
from
 
the
 
Spanish
 
and
 
they
 
may
 
have
 
experienced
 
meaning
 
change
 
over
 
the
 
centuries.
 
 
Different
 
areas
 
of
 
the
 
Cebuano
 
speaking
 
world
 
have
 
their
 
own
 
unique
 
accents
 
and
 
innovations
 
which
 
can
 
be
 
recognizable
 
to
 
speakers
 
from
 
other
 
regions.
 
It
 
is
 
similar
 
to
 
the
 
difference
 
in
 
accents
 
from
 
the
 
different
 
regions
 
of
 
the
 
USA
 
or
 
the
 
UK.
 
For
 
this
 
class,
 
we
 
will
 
primarily
 
use
 
the
 
South
 
Cebu
 
dialect
 
for
 
spelling
 
which
 
is
 
hailed
 
as
 
the
 
standard
 
by
 
many
 
language
 
organizations
 
including
 
The
 
Philippine
 
Bibles
 
Society
 
which
 
distributes
 
copies
 
of
 
the
 
Cebuano
 
Bible
 
in
 
the
 
region.
 
This
 
dialect
 
is
 
considered
 
to
 
be
 
more
 
conservative
 
and
 
lacks
 
the
 
notorious
 
“shortcuts”
 
of
 
the
 
Cebu
 
City
 
(North
 
Cebu)
 
dialect
 
and
 
is
 
used
 
widely
 
throughout
 
the
 
southern
 
Philippines.
 
It
 
is
 
also
 
the
 
preferred
 
dialect
 
in
 
generally
 
formal,
 
religious
 
and
 
political
 
settings.
 
 
There
 
can
 
be
 
marked
 
differences
 
in
 
pronunciation
 
among
 
the
 
different
 
social
 
classes.
 
Those
 
with
 
a
 
high
 
level
 
of
 
education
 
and/or
 
social
 
class
 
may
 
switch
 
in
 
and
 
out
 
of
 
English
 
frequently,
 
even
 
in
 
the
 
same
 
sentence.
 
“I
 
don’t
 
know
 
kung
 
nganong
 
ingon
 
ana
 
na
 
siyá.”
 
(I
 
don’t
 
know
 
why
 
he’s
 
like
 
that.)
 
Cebuano
 
as
 
spoken
 
in
 
the
 
countryside
 
(in
 
“the
 
province”
 
as
 
it
 
is
 
here
 
called)
 
and
 
among
 
the
 
poorer
 
classes
 
tends
 
to
 
be
 
“rougher”
 
sounding
 
and
 
there
 
is
 
much
 
less
 
English
 
mixed
 
into
 
the
 
language.
 
They
 
also
 
may
 
use
 
words
 
considered
 
“deep”
 
by
 
the
 
social
 
elites
 
of
 
the
 
Visayan
 
world.
 
 
The
 
language’s
 
variety
 
in
 
names
 
is
 
mainly
 
because
 
of
 
technically
 
overlapping
 
definitions
 
that
 
lead
 
to
 
ambiguity.
 
Many
 
have
 
been
 
proposing
 
names
 
but
 
most
 
are
 
not
 
truly
 
widely
 
accepted
 
considering
 
the
 
resistance
 
of
 
the
 
other
 
speakers
 
outside
 
of
 
Cebu.
 
Native
 
speakers
 
have
 
been
 
normally
 
calling
 
the
 
language
 
“Bisaya”
 
or
 
“Binisaya”
 
(literally
 
“the
 
Visayan
 
manner”)
 
in
 
the
 
native
 
tongue
 
which
 
translates
 
to
 
English
 
as
 
“Bisayan”
 
or
 
“Visayan”.
 
The
 
terms
 
“Cebuano”,
 
“Sinibwano”,
 
“Sinugboanon”
 
(literally
 
“of
 
the
 
Cebuano
 
manner”)
 
are
 
formally
 
used
 
to
 
distinguish
 
it
 
from
 
the
 
other
 
Visayan
 
languages
 
in
 
the
 
Visayas
 
group
 
of
 
islands
 
which
 
also
 
had
 
a
 
history
 
of
 
calling
 
themselves
 
and
 
their
 
native
 
tongues
 
“Bisaya”
 
or
 
“Binisaya”.
 
The
 
Cebuan
 
group
 
has
 
been
 
historically
 
the
 
most
 
numerous
 
among
 
the
 
Visayans
 
and
 
when
 
the
 
use
 
of
 
these
 
terms
 
died
 
down
 
in
 
the
 
others,
 
they
 
became
 
associated
 
with
 
the
 
Cebuan
 
group
 
especially
 
in
 
mainstream
 
and
 
social
 
media
 
and
 
in
 
the
 
younger
 
generations
 
who
 
were
 
not
 
able
 
to
 
witness
 
a
 
time
 
when
 
all
 
of
 
the
 
Visayans
 
identified
 
as
 
Visayan.

CONSONANTS
 
 
 
Cebuano
 
has
 
16
 
consonants
 
including
 
2
 
semivowels.
 
They
 
are
 
further
 
classified
 
with
 
articulatory
 
and
 
acoustic
 
descriptions.
 
Articulatory
 
description
 
is
 
all
 
about
 
describing
 
the
 
place
 
of
 
articulation
 
in
 
the
 
mouth.
 
Acoustic
 
description
 
describes
 
how
 
the
 
sound
 
is
 
produced
 
in
 
the
 
mouth.

&

The
 
Spanish
 
and

and
 
letters

of

Otherwise,

/dʒ/

in

/ʃ/

“ship”

as

SPANISH
 
&
 
CEBUANO
 
ORTHOGRAPHY
 
 
 
The
 
Spanish
 
left
 
a
 
lot
 
of
 
loanwords
 
in
 
Cebuano.
 
Aside
 
from
 
this,
 
traces
 
of
 
their
 
orthography
 
are
 
still
 
evident
 
today.
 
It
 
is
 
handy
 
to
 
be
 
acquainted
 
with
 
the
 
way
 
sounds
 
are
 
symbolized
 
in
 
the
 
Spanish
 
orthography
 
for
 
you
 
to
 
be
 
able
 
to
 
read
 
someone’s
 
surname,
 
the
 
name
 
of
 
a
 
town,
 
or
 
a
 
dish.
 
 
Each
 
Spanish
 
spelling
 
is
 
demonstrated
 
below
 
and
 
their
 
modern
 
Cebuano
 
equivalents
 
are
 
given
 
after.
 
There
 
hasn’t
 
been
 
a
 
universally
 
accepted
 
standard
 
orthography
 
in
 
Cebuano
 
despite
 
many
 
attempts.
 
An
 
“official”
 
one
 
assisted
 
by
 
the
 
government
 
is
 
used
 
in
 
MTB-MLE
 
(Mother
 
Tongue
 
Based
 
-
 
Multilingual
 
Education),
 
a
 
subject
 
taught
 
in
 
elementary
 
schools
 
from
 
the
 
1st
 
to
 
the
 
3rd
 
grade.
 
Choices
 
and
 
preferences
 
of
 
the
 
said
 
orthography
 
as
 
well
 
as
 
the
 
ones
 
used
 
in
 
this
 
book
 
are
 
discussed
 
below.
 
 
1.
 
I
 
and
 
Y
 
(front
 
 
 
The
 
/i/
 
sound
 
at
 
the
 
beginning
 
of
 
a
 
word
 
or
 
after
 
a
 
glottal
 
stop
 
was
 
written
 
as

or
 
Y
 
interchangeably.
 
In
 
modern
 
Cebuano,
 
this
 
is
 
consistently
 
spelled
 
as
 
I
 
except
 
when
 
it
 
is
 
after

semivowel
 
Y.
